content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
April 19,
1995
Councilmember Steve Honegger
750 Bay View Avenue
Pacific Grove, California
93950
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—95—102
Dear Councilmember Honegger:
This is in response to your request for advice under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the
“Act”) 1
QUESTION
1.
Considering that you and your wife own three houses on
the city’s Historic Resources Inventory,
may you participate in
Pacific Grove City Council decisions concerning the demolition of
historic buildings that are more than 300 feet away from the
houses you own?
2.
May you participate in city council decisions on issues
about water allocation and use that might affect your general
contracting business?
CONCLUSION
-
1.
With respect to council decisions concerning the
demolition of historic buildings that are located between 300 to
2,500 feet from real property that you own,
you may participate in
the decision, unless the decision
(a) will have a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of $10,000 or more on the fair market
value of the property you own,
or
(b) will affect the rental value
of the property by $1,000 or more per 12 month period.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—95—102
Page
2
2.
You must disqualify yourself from participating in
council decisions about water issues if the particular decision
would foreseeably result in an increase or decrease for your
general contracting business of gross revenues of $10,000 per
fiscal year,
expenses of $2,500 per fiscal year, or assets or
liabilities of $10,000.
In addition,
you must refrain from
participating in decisions affecting individuals or businesses
that are sources of income to you through your general contracting
business and your ownership of real property.
FACTS
You and your wife own three houses on the city’s Historic
Resources Inventory.
You have been advised by the city attorney
for Pacific Grove that you could not participate in the drafting
or approval of the Historic Preservation Ordinance.
You have also
refrained from participating in council decisions on demolition
applications involving houses on the list that are within 300 feet
of your houses.
However,
you now ask whether you may participate
in council decisions regarding the demolition of houses that are
more than 300 feet away from the houses that you own.
In addition,
you are the sole owner of a general contracting
business.
Typically one to three employees work for you in your
business.
As a general contractor, you mostly construct home
additions,
and remodel kitchens and bathrooms,
although you have
built several new homes in Pacific Grove.
You take out building
permits yourself, as well as water permits from the Water
Management District,
on jobs where such permits are required.
About 90 percent of your general contracting work is within the
city of Pacific Grove.
The general contracting jobs you undertake
are usually worth between $20,000 and $50,000 each.
The Monterey Peninsula has a serious water shortage.
A
Regional Water Management District has beefl established to
allocate limited water supplies to local jurisdictions and to seek
new water sources.
The Pacific Grove City Council recently had to
decide on a self—imposed moratorium on the issuance of building
permits that resulted in increased water use.
You did not
participate in this decision based on my telephone advice that as
a general contractor, you might have a conflict.
The council is currently faced with numerous decisions on
water issues as described in the Report of the Joint Planning
Commission/City Council Committee on water issues dated March 15,
1995.
Water issues discussed in the report include:
changing the
number of city water allocation categories, placing certain water
in the City Administered Comiuunity Reserve,
adopting a fixture
unit allocation system for future projects which create water
demand (each new dwelling would be allocated water based on the
set number of baths, sinks,
and other fixtures they are
permitted), reviewing the water allocation process,
and changing
the way water and building permits are issued.


File No.
A—95—102
Page
3
The council will continue to have water issues come before it
as allocations change and new water becomes available.
You
question whether you may participate in decisions about these
water issues if they might affect your general contracting
business.
ANALYSIS
1.
Economic Interests
The Political Reform Act prohibits any. public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
(Section 87100.)
A
“public official” is defined as a member, officer,
employee,
or
consultant of a state or local government agency.
(Section 82048;
Regulation 18700.)
As a Pacific Grove City Councilmember, you are
considered a “public official” under the Act.
Section 87103 of the Act provides that:
A public official has a financial interest in
a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally,
on the
official,
a member of his or her immediate family,
or on any of the following:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect intbrest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or
more in value provided to, received by or promised
to the public official within 12 months prior to
the time when the decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.


File No.
A—95—102
Page
4
The houses you own and your general contracting business both
constitute financial interests.
The three houses you and your
wife own on the city’s Historic Resources Inventory constitute
interests in real property worth more than $1,000 under subsection
(b)
above.
Your general contracting business is a financial
interest under subsections (a),
(c),
and
(d)
above, since it is a
business in which you have an investment of more than $1,000, it
is a source of income to you of substantially more than $250,
and
you manage the business.
Accordingly, you may not participate in
decisions which will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on your real property interests or your general
contracting business.
2.
Foreseeability and Materiality
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
The effect of a
decision is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not
required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is
not reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
a.
Real Property
Regulation 18702.3(a) states that the effect of a decision is
material with respect to real property an official owns if:
(1)
The real property in which the
official has an interest,
or any part of that
real property, is located within a 300 foot
radius of the boundaries
(or the proposed
boundaries)
of the property which is the
subject of the decision, unless the decision
will have no financial effect upon the
official’s real property interest.
(3)
The real property in which the
official has an interest is located outside a
radius of 300 feet and any part of the real
property is located within a radius of 2,500
feet of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries)
of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of:


File No.
A—95—102
Page 5
(A)
Ten thousand dollars
($10,000)
or
more on the fair market value of the real
property in which the official has an
interest; or
(B)
Will affect the rental value of the
property by $1,000 or more per 12 month
period.
Thus, if any of the properties you own are within 300 feet of
real property that is the subject of a governmental decision, the
effect of the decision on the property you own is considered
material unless the decision would have no financial effect on the
property.
Under this standard,
you should continue to disqualify
yourself from participating in council decisions about the
demolition of buildings that are within 300 feet of any of the
three houses which you own.
Under the regulation, however, you
may participate in council decisions about the demolition of
historic buildings that are located a distance of over 300 feet to
2,500 feet away from any of the houses that you own, unless the
decision will have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of
$10,000 or more on the fair market value of the houses you own,
or
will affect the rental value of the houses by $1,000 or more per
12 month period.
b.
General Contracting Business
Your general contracting business is not directly involved in
council decisions about water issues, but may be indirectly
affected.
Regulation 18702.2 applies to business entities that
are indirectly involved in a decision.
Under Regulation
18702.2(g), the effect of a decision is material as to a business
entity in which an official has an economic interest if:
(1)
The decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year of
$10,000 or more; or
(2)
The decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or
eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $2,500 or more; or
(3)
The decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of
$10,000 or more.
If decisions regarding particular water issues would
foreseeably cause the gross revenues of your general contracting


File No.
A—95—102
Page
6
business to increase or decrease by $10,000 per fiscal year, cause
expenses to increase or decrease by $2,500 per fiscal year, or
cause the assets or liabilities to increase or decrease by
$10,000,
you must disqualify yourself from participating in the
decisions.
(See Skousen Advice Letter
1
No.
A—87-062..)
Whether a council decision on a particular water issue will
foreseeably cause any of the above financial effects on your
business is a factual question for you to determine.
Under this
standard, decisions about certain water issues,
such as whether to
consolidate the number of city water allocation categories or
whether to allocate excess water to the community reserve, might
not have any of the above financial effects on your business.
But
other water decisions,
such as removing the moratorium on issuing
building permits that increase water use,
changing the building
and water permit issuing system, or adopting a fixture unit
allocation system for new dwellings that create water demand,
might easily have a financial effect on your contracting business
as described above.
Since the contracting jobs you undertake are
usually worth between $20,000 and $50,000, if it is foreseeable
that a particular water decision might result in your having one
less job a year, it would affect your business’ gross revenues by
over $10,000 and you would be required to disqualify yourself from
participating in the decision.
c.
Sources of Income
In addition to the conflicts discussed above which may arise
as a result of your real property interests and general
contracting business, you may possibly have other conflicts with
respect to individuals or businesses that are sources of income to
you.
Section 87103(c)
of the Act set forth above provides that an
official’s financial interest includes any source of income of
$250 or more received by or promised to the official within 12
months before a decision affecting that source is made.
If you
sell or rent any of the three houses you own, the buyers or
renters would be considered sources of income to you.
Any
individuals or businesses for whom you have done general
contracting work would also be sources of income to you.
The Act
requires that you disqualify yourself from decisions if it is
reasonably foreseeable that they would have a material financial
effect on any of these individuals or businesses as well.
Public officials who have a financial interest in a
governmental decision must not only disqualify themselves from
voting on those decisions, but also must refrain from any
participation in connection with those decisions.
This includes
advising or making recommendations to other council members or
otherwise attempting to influence the decision.


File No.
A—95--102
Page
7
I trust this answers your question.
If you have any further
questions regarding this matter, please contact me at 916/322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Hyla P. Wagner
Counsel, Legal Division

about water allocation and use that might affect your general

CONCLUSION: -
1.

value of the property you own,

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission April 19, 1995 Councilmember Steve Honegger 750 Bay View Avenue Pacific Grove, California 93950 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A—95—102 Dear Councilmember Honegger: This is in response to your request for advice under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”) 1 QUESTION 1. Considering that you and your wife own three houses on the city’s Historic Resources Inventory, may you participate in Pacific Grove City Council decisions concerning the demolition of historic buildings that are more than 300 feet away from the houses you own? 2. May you participate in city council decisions on issues about water allocation and use that might affect your general contracting business? CONCLUSION - 1. With respect to council decisions concerning the demolition of historic buildings that are located between 300 to 2,500 feet from real property that you own, you may participate in the decision, unless the decision (a) will have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of $10,000 or more on the fair market value of the property you own, or (b) will affect the rental value of the property by $1,000 or more per 12 month period. 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000—18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 File No. A—95—102 Page 2 2. You must disqualify yourself from participating in council decisions about water issues if the particular decision would foreseeably result in an increase or decrease for your general contracting business of gross revenues of $10,000 per fiscal year, expenses of $2,500 per fiscal year, or assets or liabilities of $10,000. In addition, you must refrain from participating in decisions affecting individuals or businesses that are sources of income to you through your general contracting business and your ownership of real property. FACTS You and your wife own three houses on the city’s Historic Resources Inventory. You have been advised by the city attorney for Pacific Grove that you could not participate in the drafting or approval of the Historic Preservation Ordinance. You have also refrained from participating in council decisions on demolition applications involving houses on the list that are within 300 feet of your houses. However, you now ask whether you may participate in council decisions regarding the demolition of houses that are more than 300 feet away from the houses that you own. In addition, you are the sole owner of a general contracting business. Typically one to three employees work for you in your business. As a general contractor, you mostly construct home additions, and remodel kitchens and bathrooms, although you have built several new homes in Pacific Grove. You take out building permits yourself, as well as water permits from the Water Management District, on jobs where
